items.foco: Tetanus toxin inhibits release of GABA and glycine
items.title: Mechanism of tetanus toxin
items.contentHtml:
Tetanus toxin: inhibition of GABA and glycine

  • Tetanus toxin (tetanospasmin) is a metalloprotease that cleaves synaptobrevin (VAMP), blocking vesicular exocytosis. Inhibits the release of GABA and glycine in inhibitory interneurons of the spinal anterior horn.
  • Physiopathology: recurrent loss of inhibition → spasticity and simultaneous muscle contraction (opisthotonus).
  • The toxin travels by retrograde axonal transport from the wound to the CNS; It does not cross the blood-brain barrier.
  • Term: Tetanospasmin: Clostridium tetani toxin, responsible for the neurological clinical picture.

items.foco: Clostridium tetani causes spasticity and lockjaw
items.title: Clinical picture of tetanus
items.contentHtml:
Tetanus: spasticity and lockjaw

  • Clostridium tetani causes generalized spasticity and lockjaw (masseter rigidity) as the first symptom in >50% of cases.
  • Course: sardonic laughter (risus sardonicus), opisthotonos, dysphagia, spasms triggered by minimal stimuli.
  • Clinical diagnosis: no cultures required; the injury may be trivial. Classification: generalized tetanus (most common), localized, cephalic and neonatal.
  • DDx: strychnine poisoning (similar but without lockjaw or risus sardonicus) and hypocalcemic tetany.

items.title: Mechanism of botulinum toxin
items.contentHtml:
Botulinum toxin: acetylcholine blockade

  • Botulinum toxin (serotypes A-G, human A, B, E, F) is a zinc-dependent endopeptidase that cleaves SNARE proteins (SNAP-25, syntaxin, VAMP). Blocks the release of acetylcholine in the motor plate.
  • Result: flaccid paralysis without affecting sensitivity or consciousness. There are no fasciculations.
  • The toxin is absorbed in the intestine or wound, it reaches the neuromuscular junction via hematogenous means.
  • Term: SNARE: family of proteins necessary for the fusion of synaptic vesicles; Its cleavage prevents the exocytosis of acetylcholine.

items.foco: Clostridium botulinum causes descending flaccid paralysis
items.title: Botulism: descending paralysis
items.contentHtml:
Botulism: descending flaccid paralysis

  • Clostridium botulinum produces symmetrical descending flaccid paralysis, which begins in the cranial nerves (diplopia, dysphagia, ptosis) and progresses to the respiratory muscles.
  • There is no fever or sensory alteration; decreased or abolished deep tendon reflexes.
  • Forms: dietary, infantile (due to spores in honey), wound and iatrogenic botulism.
  • Diagnosis: detection of toxin in serum/feces by mouse bioassay (gold standard).
  • DDx: Guillain-Barré syndrome (Miller Fisher variant), myasthenia gravis, poisoning organophosphates.

items.foco: Clostridium difficile produces toxins A and B
items.title: C. difficile toxins
items.contentHtml:
C. difficile: toxins A and B

  • Clostridium difficile produces two exotoxins: toxin A (enterotoxin, attracts neutrophils) and toxin B (cytotoxin, more potent, causes direct cellular damage).
  • Both are glucosyltransferases that inactivate Rho-GTPases → cytoskeletal disorganization, apoptosis and loss of integrity of the epithelial barrier.
  • The hypervirulent strain (ribotype 027) produces toxins A and B in greater quantities, in addition to binary toxin (CDT), associated with greater mortality and recurrence.
  • Diagnosis: detection of free toxins in feces (ELISA) or glutamate dehydrogenase (GDH) test plus confirmation by toxin.

items.foco: Colonic pseudomembranes caused by C. difficile
items.title: Pseudomembranous colitis
items.contentHtml:
Colonic pseudomembranes

  • Colonic pseudomembranes caused by C. difficile are raised yellowish plaques of fibrin, mucus and neutrophils that cover the mucosa of the colon, visible in colonoscopy.
  • Characteristic finding in pseudomembranous colitis, although it may be absent in mild forms (colitis without pseudomembranes).
  • Most frequent location: descending colon and rectosigmoid; It can be patched.
  • Procedure: Colonoscopy is the gold standard for visual diagnosis, but not always necessary; flexible sigmoidoscopy is sufficient if there is high suspicion.

items.foco: Metronidazole or oral vancomycin for C. difficile colitis
items.title: C. difficile colitis treatment
items.contentHtml:
Treatment: metronidazole and oral vancomycin

  • First line for mild-moderate infection: metronidazole 500 mg PO every 8 hours for 10-14 days.
  • Severe or recurrent infection: oral vancomycin 125 mg PO every 6 hours (not IV, does not reach intestinal lumen).
  • For fulminant: oral vancomycin plus IV metronidazole, and consider colectomy if ileus or toxic megacolon.
  • New options: fidaxomicin 200 mg PO every 12 hours, with a lower recurrence rate than vancomycin.
  • Examination trap: do not use antidiarrheals (loperamide) because they retain toxins and worsen the box.

items.foco: Tetanus antitoxin does not neutralize intracellular toxin
items.title: Limitation of tetanus antitoxin
items.contentHtml:
Tetanus antitoxin: intracellular ineffectiveness

  • Tetanus antitoxin (human tetanus immunoglobulin, 3000-5000 IU IM) neutralizes only extracellular free toxin; it does not neutralize intracellular toxin already internalized in the axon.
  • Therefore, its administration must be early (before the toxin enters the neurons). Once fixed in the CNS, the damage is irreversible and the treatment is supportive.
  • Indication in dirty or risky wounds (puncture wounds, with dirt, >6 hours of evolution): administer antitoxin plus tetanus vaccine (double immunization).
  • Trap: the antitoxin does not reverse the established condition, it only prevents progression.

items.title: Tetanus vaccine
items.contentHtml:
Tetanus vaccine: primary prevention

  • The tetanus vaccine (tetanus toxoid) is a toxin inactivated with formaldehyde, which induces protective IgG antibodies (anti-toxin).
  • Primary schedule: 3 doses (at 2, 4 and 6 months) plus boosters at 18 months, 4-6 years and then every 10 years.
  • In tetanus wounds: if >5 years have passed since the last dose, administer reinforcement; if >10 years or not vaccinated: vaccine plus tetanus immunoglobulin.
  • Combined presentations: DTPa (diphtheria, tetanus, acellular pertussis), Td (adults) or Tdap (adolescents and adults).
  • Term: Tetanus toxoid: detoxified form of the toxin that retains antigenicity without toxicity.

items.foco: Infant botulism due to spores in honey
items.title: Infant botulism and honey
items.contentHtml:
Infant botulism: spores in honey

  • Infant botulism occurs in infants <12 months due to ingestion of spores in honey that germinate in the immature colon and produce toxin in vivo.
  • Clinical symptoms: initial constipation, followed by hypotonia, weak crying, poor suction, ptosis and descending paralysis (“flaccid baby”).
  • Diagnosis: detection of toxin or spores in feces (not in serum, because the toxin is produced locally).
  • Prevention: do not administer honey to children under 1 year of age. Treatment: intravenous human botulinum immunoglobulin (BabyBIG), reduces hospital stay.
  • Trap: equine antitoxin is not used in children due to the risk of hypersensitivity; BabyBIG is preferred.

items.foco: Botulinum toxin used in strabismus and dystonia
items.title: Therapeutic uses of botulinum toxin
items.contentHtml:
Botulinum toxin: clinical applications

  • Botulinum toxin type A (Botox, Dysport) is used in strabismus (weakening overactive extraocular muscles) and focal dystonias (blepharospasm, spasmodic torticollis, laryngeal dystonia).
  • Other indications: post-stroke spasticity, axillary hyperhidrosis, chronic migraine, overactive bladder.
  • Reversible effect (3-6 months) due to regeneration of nerve terminals.
  • Dose: varies according to indication; in strabismus 1.25-5 IU per muscle; in cervical dystonia 200-400 IU total.
  • Trap: do not use in disorders of the neuromuscular junction (myasthenia gravis, Eaton-Lambert) due to the risk of enhancing paralysis.

items.foco: C. difficile associated with the use of broad-spectrum antibiotics
items.title: Risk factors for C. difficile
items.contentHtml:
C. difficile: association with antibiotics

  • C. difficile is associated with use of broad-spectrum antibiotics that alter the intestinal microbiota, especially clindamycin, cephalosporins, fluoroquinolones and penicillins.
  • Other factors: prolonged hospitalization, age >65 years, immunosuppression, use of proton pump inhibitors (PPIs).
  • Diarrhea typically appears during or up to 8 weeks after the antibiotic. Not every patient on antibiotics and diarrhea has C. difficile; confirmation with toxin testing is required.
  • Prevention: antibiotic restriction, hand hygiene with soap and water (alcohol does not eliminate spores), contact isolation.
